questions.question: What problem does Centralized Training, Decentralized Execution (CTDE) solve?
options: GPU memory fragmentation

Independent per-agent RL is non-stationary (everyone else is learning too), and fully centralized control doesn't scale or match deployment — CTDE trains with global information but deploys local-only policies
Slow network synchronization
Reward sparsity in single-agent RL
questions.explanation: The three-way trade is the heart of MARL: independent learning sees a moving target, centralized control violates execution constraints, CTDE gets both — critics see everything at training time, actors see only their own observations at test time.

questions.question: In MADDPG, what do the actor and critic each see?
options: Both see everything at all times
The actor maps only its own observation to action; the critic sees ALL agents' observations and actions during training to reduce variance — and is discarded at deployment
The critic sees nothing; the actor sees everything
Both see only local observations
questions.explanation: That asymmetry IS CTDE. The known failure mode: critic input grows with N agents, so MADDPG doesn't scale past ~10 agents without approximation.

questions.question: What does QMIX's monotonic mixing constraint buy?
options: Faster wall-clock training
With df/dQ_i ≥ 0, argmax of the joint Q distributes — each agent independently maximizing its own Q maximizes the team Q, which is exactly the decentralized-execution property
Compatibility with continuous actions
Provable convergence to global optimum
questions.explanation: Value decomposition with monotonic mixing is why QMIX dominated SMAC's cooperative micro-management. The cost: non-monotone reward structures (one agent sacrificing for the team) break the constraint — hence QTRAN/QPLEX extensions.

questions.question: Why did MAPPO become the default 2026 cooperative-MARL baseline?
options: It was the first MARL algorithm ever

PPO with a centralized value function matched or beat off-policy MARL across particle-world, SMAC, Football, and Hanabi with minimal tuning and stable, reproducible training — the community had underrated on-policy MARL
It requires no value function at all
It is the only one with a public implementation
questions.explanation: Yu et al. 2022's "surprisingly effective" result reset the field's defaults: any new cooperative-MARL method now has to beat MAPPO, not just MADDPG/QMIX.

questions.question: Why does MARL still matter for LLM-agent systems in 2026?
options: LLMs are trained with QMIX internally

Teams increasingly train coordination policies — when to defer, when to act, which peer to call — and CTDE, value decomposition, and centralized critics are the pattern vocabulary for doing that with decentralized execution
MARL replaces prompting entirely
It doesn't — MARL is purely historical
questions.explanation: The MARL literature predates the LLM wave but answers its coordination-training questions. Reading those papers without the CTDE vocabulary is painful; with it, the design space is three patterns.
